title: Medication continuity bag
summary: A go-ready medicine information bag with labels, pharmacy contacts, refill timing, and storage notes.
content: Disasters interrupt medicine through lost packaging, heat, closed pharmacies, evacuation, or confusion between similar pills. The bag protects exact names, original labels, prescriber, pharmacy, allergies, storage needs, refill dates, and emergency contacts. It does not replace medical advice.
steps: Write medicine names exactly from labels.
Keep dosage instructions from labels or prescriptions, not memory.
Note refrigeration or heat protection needs.
Check refill dates weekly during unstable periods.
warnings: Do not change doses or substitute medicines without qualified advice.
Do not store heat-sensitive medicine in a hot car or balcony.
Do not mix different people's loose pills.
common_mistakes: Only one person knows the plan.
Keeping prescriptions only on a dead phone.
alternatives: Run a smaller version of Medication continuity bag with one responsible person, one helper, and one visible paper note.
Use a safer fallback location or method for Medication continuity bag if the first choice becomes wet, crowded, contaminated, dark, unstable, or unsafe.
Pause Medication continuity bag and verify the written record with a second person before moving scarce supplies.
failure_signs: People cannot explain who is responsible for Medication continuity bag or when the next check happens.
Supplies connected to Medication continuity bag are unlabelled, damp, contaminated, damaged, missing, or being used for the wrong purpose.
The routine ignores water, hygiene, food contact, and waste boundaries and starts creating new risk instead of reducing existing risk during “Medication continuity bag”
when_not_to_use: Do not use Medication continuity bag as a reason to delay evacuation, emergency calls, medical care, fire response, or official local instructions.
Do not use Medication continuity bag when the location is structurally unsafe, chemically suspicious, smoky, flooded, violent, or otherwise immediately dangerous.
Do not improvise hazardous repairs, dosing, chemical handling, weapon use, or electrical/gas work under the label of Medication continuity bag.
short_term: For the first hours of Medication continuity bag, keep the action small, visible, and reversible: write the goal, assign one owner, protect vulnerable people, separate safe from unsafe supplies, and set a review time.
long_term: If Medication continuity bag remains necessary for days or weeks, rotate roles, preserve written lessons, replace fragile supplies, and train another person so the group does not depend on one expert.
if_method_fails: If Medication continuity bag fails, stop escalation, move to the safest fallback, record what failed, protect people first, and restart only with a simpler written plan.
environment_notes: Adapt Medication continuity bag to the real environment: check water, hygiene, food contact, and waste boundaries, local weather, building condition, water access, light, noise, animals, neighbours, and safe exits before repeating the routine.
